name: Oaxaca
summary: Oaxaca is considered one of Mexico's safer states with lower violent crime rates than the national average. However, the city has experienced increased street muggings since 2021, particularly affecting tourists in the historic center.
quickVerdict: Oaxaca remains relatively safe for tourists compared to other Mexican destinations, with violent crime against visitors being rare. The main concerns are petty crime including street muggings, pickpocketing in markets, and some reports of robberies during evening hours. While the state avoided major cartel conflicts affecting other regions, travelers should exercise standard precautions, avoid displaying valuables, and be especially cautious when walking alone after dark.

neighborhoods.name: Centro Historico (Zocalo Area)
neighborhoods.description: The heart of Oaxaca with heavy foot traffic, police presence, and most tourist attractions. Generally safe during day and early evening hours, with many people around providing natural security.

neighborhoods.name: Jalatlaco
neighborhoods.description: Trendy neighborhood popular with solo travelers, known for vibrant street art and family atmosphere. Well-maintained area with good lighting and local community presence.

neighborhoods.name: Xochimilco
neighborhoods.description: Quiet residential area with colorful murals and family-friendly atmosphere. Popular accommodation choice for those wanting to stay central but in a calmer setting.

neighborhoods.name: Reforma
neighborhoods.description: Mixed residential and commercial area with decent safety record. Generally safe during daylight hours but requires more caution at night.

neighborhoods.name: Outskirts of Centro
neighborhoods.description: Edge areas of the historic center that become quiet during evenings and holidays. Higher risk of street crime, especially on Sundays when streets are less populated.

neighborhoods.name: Istmo Region (Salina Cruz area)
neighborhoods.description: Remote southeastern region of Oaxaca state experiencing recent security issues with reports of blockades and civil unrest. Most tourists do not visit this area.

scams.name: Overpriced Taxi Fares
scams.description: Taxi drivers may quote inflated prices to tourists or refuse to use meters, especially near tourist attractions and hotels.
scams.howToAvoid: Always negotiate fare before getting in, use DiDi app when available, or ask your hotel to arrange trusted transportation.

scams.name: Fake Tour Operators
scams.description: Street vendors around the Zocalo offer tours to sites like Hierve el Agua or Monte Alban that may be poorly organized or overpriced.
scams.howToAvoid: Book tours through reputable agencies, GetYourGuide, or your hotel concierge. Read recent reviews before booking any tour.

scams.name: Market Overcharging
scams.description: Vendors in tourist markets may quote higher prices for crafts, textiles, or food items when they identify tourists.
scams.howToAvoid: Learn basic Spanish numbers, observe what locals pay, and don't hesitate to politely negotiate. Walk away if prices seem unreasonable.

scams.name: ATM Card Skimming
scams.description: Criminals may install devices on ATMs to steal card information, particularly at machines outside banks or in tourist areas.
scams.howToAvoid: Use ATMs inside banks or major hotels, cover your PIN when entering, and check your statements regularly for unauthorized transactions.

soloFemale.overview: Oaxaca is considered one of the safest places in Mexico for solo female travelers, with many women reporting positive experiences. The city has less machismo culture compared to other Mexican destinations, and locals are generally respectful. However, standard precautions should be taken, especially regarding street crime and nighttime safety.
tips: Stay in central neighborhoods like Jalatlaco or Xochimilco for better safety and community presence
Take taxis or DiDi rides after 10pm rather than walking alone, even in the centro area
Keep valuables secure and avoid displaying phones, cameras, or jewelry conspicuously in public
Trust your instincts and remove yourself from situations that feel uncomfortable
Connect with other female travelers at hostels or through social media groups for companionship

nightSafety.overview: While Oaxaca's nightlife areas around the Zocalo and Santo Domingo Church remain relatively safe with good lighting and people present, crime does increase at night. The surge in street muggings since 2021 has included some incidents during late afternoon and evening hours. Most areas become quieter after 11pm.
tips: Stick to well-lit, populated areas like the Zocalo and walking street between Zocalo and Santo Domingo
Always take a taxi or DiDi back to your accommodation after 10pm, even for short distances
Avoid walking alone on side streets or in peripheral areas of the centro after dark
Let someone know your plans and expected return time when going out at night
Limit alcohol consumption to maintain awareness of your surroundings

transport.metro: Oaxaca does not have a metro system. Public buses run on main routes for 8-15 pesos but can be crowded. Keep bags secure and verify stops with other passengers.
transport.rideshare: DiDi (similar to Uber) operates in Oaxaca and is generally safer than street taxis. Download the app and verify license plates. Uber is not available in the city.
transport.taxis: Official taxis are available but negotiate fare beforehand as meters are often not used. Take photos of license plates for safety. Hotel-arranged taxis are typically more reliable.
transport.tips: For safety, avoid unmarked or unofficial taxis, especially late at night. Walking is feasible in the compact historic center during daylight hours.

customs: Greet everyone individually with 'Buenos dias/tardes' and a handshake rather than addressing groups collectively
Use formal 'usted' when speaking Spanish to elders, strangers, or in professional settings
Remove hats and dress modestly when entering churches, covering shoulders and wearing long pants
Tip 10-15% in restaurants and small amounts to street musicians or vendors as a gesture of appreciation
Say 'con permiso' when passing by someone or reaching over them in crowded spaces like markets

health.overview: Oaxaca has adequate healthcare facilities in the city center, though quality varies. Private hospitals offer better care for tourists but require upfront payment. The state historically lags in health indicators compared to Mexico's national average.
health.water: Tap water is not safe to drink. Use bottled water or garrafones (5-gallon jugs) available at most accommodations. Most restaurants and street food vendors use purified water and ice.
health.vaccinations: No special vaccinations required beyond routine immunizations. Consider hepatitis A and typhoid vaccines. Consult a travel clinic at least one month before departure.
health.altitude: Oaxaca sits at 1,555 meters (5,100 feet) elevation. Some visitors may experience mild altitude effects. Stay hydrated and avoid excessive alcohol initially. The climate is generally mild year-round.

emergency.usEmbassy: US Embassy Mexico City: +52-55-2579-2000. Consular services available. For US citizens in Oaxaca, contact Mexico City embassy for assistance.

faq.a: Yes, Oaxaca is generally safe for tourists and considered one of Mexico's safer destinations. The main risks are petty crimes like street muggings and pickpocketing rather than violent crime. Exercise normal precautions and stay aware of your surroundings.

The historic center areas around Zocalo and Santo Domingo remain relatively safe at night with good lighting and people present, but crime does increase after dark. Take taxis for transportation after 10pm and avoid quiet side streets.

faq.q: Is Oaxaca safe for solo female travelers?
faq.a: Oaxaca is considered one of the safest places in Mexico for solo female travelers, with less machismo culture than other regions. Many women travel here successfully alone, though standard precautions about nighttime safety and securing valuables still apply.

faq.q: What areas should I avoid in Oaxaca?
faq.a: Avoid the remote Istmo region near Salina Cruz due to recent security issues. In the city, be cautious on the outskirts of centro, especially quiet side streets during evenings and holidays when foot traffic is minimal.

faq.q: Is it safe to use public transport in Oaxaca?
faq.a: Local buses are generally safe but can be crowded - keep bags secure. DiDi rideshare is recommended over street taxis. The historic center is compact and walkable during daylight hours, which is often the safest transportation option.
